Document [7] (Title: Le Fort Fractures -- Introduction) A combination of blows with a wooden club and a metal rod, stomping on the face, crushing with a vise, and "hurling" heads at the edge of a table was employed by his aides, after which the skulls were boiled to facilitate removal of the soft tissue for subsequent osseous examination. [1] [2] Despite the lack of consistency in the experimental protocol to force, orientation, and mechanism of injury, Le Fort's work described many patterns in which fractures most commonly occur along the “lines of least resistance” ( linea minoris resistentiae ) that he identified (see Images . Facial Great Lines of Weakness, Profile View and Facial Great Lines of Weakness, Front View). [2] These patterns are now classified into 3 types based on their orientation: horizontal (type I), pyramidal (type II), and transverse (type III). All of these injuries separate the upper jaw from the skull and, by definition, involve fractures of the pterygoid plates of the sphenoid bone. The severe pattern fractures (pyramidal and transverse) occur more superiorly than the horizontal fracture, separating more of the facial skeleton from the neurocranium. These fractures are typically the result of greater forces applied to the central and upper midface.

Document [9] (Title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Partial pulpotomy) Partial pulpotomy involves amputating the pulp 2 mm below the exposure site, where the tissue is presumed to be healthy. [16] This technique is the treatment of choice for young teeth with open apices when pulp capping is contraindicated, such as when excessive time has elapsed since the trauma or when the exposure diameter is too large. Partial pulpotomy serves as a provisional yet long-term approach, allowing the tooth to remain vital for as long as possible. However, root canal treatment followed by appropriate restoration may eventually be required. Restorative options include freehand or indirect composite resin restoration, fragment reattachment if available, and ceramic restoration.

Document [11] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction) Stainless steel crowns are the most commonly used restorative option for repairing and preserving the remaining tissue of severely damaged and decayed teeth (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown). They were introduced into pediatric dentistry in 1947, first described by Engel, and then popularised by Humphrey in 1950. Stainless steel crowns have outperformed other materials, such as amalgam and composite, in terms of durability and longevity for more than a half-century. In fact, no restorative material has provided the benefits of low cost, reliability, and durability when interim full-coronal coverage is required. [3] [4]

Document [14] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance) Primary teeth are relatively at a larger risk of caries than permanent teeth. They also have larger pulp with prominent pulp horns, which places exact demands on cavity design. Hence, a full-coverage restoration is required. Although stainless steel crowns have an aesthetic disadvantage, they are still the treatment of choice for the following reasons: Durability Efficiency Longevity Cost-effectiveness Reliability Even though other tooth-colored restorations are available in the market, they are costly or require more tooth reduction, increasing the chances of pulp exposure. Document [16] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation) A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is the preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]

Fracture care with hardware has been evolving as we gain more experience.  In the 1970s through the 1990s, rigid constructs (stainless steel) were preferred.  In the past 20 years, there has been a shift away from "complete rigidity." Document [25] (Title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures) Crown-root fractures are initially managed by identifying the extent of the fracture and exposing its margins. Document [27] (Title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures) The primary challenge in the restorative management of crown-root fractures is ensuring restorability, as subgingival margins often necessitate additional treatment to expose more tooth structure and establish a ferrule. Document [30] (Title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance) The restorations are processed either by soft machining of pre-sintered blanks or by hard machining of fully sintered blocks. The mechanical properties of 3Y-TZP are strongly affected by its grain size. The mechanical properties of zirconia have been reported to be higher than other ceramics for dental applications. Fracture resistance of 6–10 MPa/m1/2, a flexural strength of 900–1200 MPa, and a compression resistance of 2000 MPa have been reported. Zirconia restorations bear an average load of 755 N. Zirconia yields higher fracture loads than alumina or lithium disilicate. Zirconia ceramics yield superior wear behavior and lower antagonistic wear compared to porcelain.
Document [31] (Title: Elbow Fractures Overview -- Treatment / Management -- Interventions for Supracondylar Fractures) Closed reduction with percutaneous pinning is a standard technique in managing displaced fractures. Bone fragments are realigned through closed reduction and stabilized with 2 lateral metal pins. Three pins may be required in the presence of severe displacement or a floating distal segment. The fixation is reinforced with a splint or cast, and both the pins and immobilization device are removed once adequate healing is observed in the ensuing weeks.

Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823) Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754) A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 167) Additional concepts that belong to this SMQ include تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip‑head prosthesis), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures), all of which are clinical entities commonly managed in the setting of osteoporotic bone. Together, these relationships illustrate how various closed fractures—whether of the femur, skull, humerus, scapula, or ulna—are interconnected through shared morphology, common etiologic links to osteoporosis, and overlapping classifications within intracranial and skeletal injury hierarchies, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding injury patterns and guiding patient‑centered management.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877) The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” and “Trochanterplasty,” which address femoral implant revision, comprehensive knee arthroplasty component exchange, and greater trochanter reshaping to improve hip biomechanics. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the extensive, femur‑centered surgical repertoire for managing growth disturbances, fractures, malunions, deformities, and prosthetic complications, emphasizing the need for individualized operative planning based on anatomical site, underlying pathology, and desired functional outcome.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122) The procedural category **THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS** encompasses a comprehensive set of operative interventions that address fractures, dislocations, deformities, limb‑length discrepancies, prosthetic failures, and reconstructive needs of the hip, femur, tibia, fibula, and adjacent lower‑extremity structures. Direct‑site femoral procedures include **internal fixation of femur** (with or without fracture reduction), **intramedullary nailing of femur**, **open reduction of fracture of femur** (with or without internal fixation), **femoral shortening with blade plate**, **repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck**, and various **osteoplasties of femur** (plain, lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with femoral segment transfer). **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur.
